world_simulation.philosophy: The world runs whether or not the player is watching. Every system described here operates continuously in the background. The player is one actor in a world full of actors.

economic_simulation.core_loop: Resources extracted → processed → traded → consumed → destroyed. No source is infinite. Scarcity is real and emergent.
tracked_variables: Total currency in circulation by region
Food production vs consumption by region and settlement
Resource stock levels (iron ore, timber, grain, peat, fish, etc.)
Price levels per commodity per region

currency_circulation.can_be_destroyed: Melted down, lost in disasters, buried and not recovered
currency_circulation.can_be_created: New mining of ore — slow and limited
currency_circulation.hoarding_effects: NPCs hoarding coin reduces local liquidity, affecting price and trade activity
currency_circulation.war_consumption: Armed conflict destroys steel currency (melted for weapons) and generally disrupts circulation

population_simulation.birth_rate: Slow replenishment. Population grows in stable prosperous conditions. Declines rapidly in war/plague/famine.
population_simulation.migration: NPCs will leave dangerous or economically failed settlements for better opportunities elsewhere. This is tracked.
population_simulation.labor_pool_effects: War kills farmers → food production drops → prices rise → unrest → political instability. The chain is real.
population_simulation.npc_age_progression: NPCs age in real time. Old NPCs die. Children grow up. Apprentices become journeymen become masters.

faction_autonomy.factions_pursue_goals: All factions have resources, ambitions, and friction with each other. They pursue their goals on their own schedule regardless of player involvement.
examples: Brekhard the Younger is moving on Cravenmoor's Ford whether the player is involved or not
Queen Aldeth's health is declining on a real timer
The Dust Council succession crisis resolves one way or another
The Karn Clan negotiations in Thornsveld proceed or fail
faction_autonomy.coalition_forming: Factions can form alliances autonomously based on shared interests, common enemies, trade relationships

season_effects.spring: Planting. Food reserves at their lowest. Roads muddy and slow. Energy and hope.
season_effects.summer: Growing. Most active trade period. Campaigns and military action peak. Longest days.
season_effects.autumn: Harvest. Most food available. Preservation work critical. Political settlements before winter.
season_effects.winter: Survival. Food from stores. Travel dangerous. Military action minimal. The real killer of the unprepared.

seasonal_system.winter_crisis_potential: If autumn harvest failed or stores were depleted by war/plague/poor management, winter kills. This affects NPCs, factions, and the player equally.
seasonal_system.disease_seasonality: Disease spikes in late summer (contaminated water, heat) and winter (cold, malnutrition, crowding).

disease_system.diseases_are_real: Plague, fever, respiratory illness, waterborne disease — all modeled.
disease_system.transmission: Disease spreads through population via contact, contaminated water, poor hygiene, and proximity to the sick.
disease_system.war_and_disease: Armies concentrate disease. The Coughing Black traveled with the Ashen March armies. This dynamic repeats.
disease_system.named_current_threat: The unidentified illness in the Free Fens — possibly linked to the deep fen contamination near Varathi ruins.
disease_system.hygiene_effects: Player and NPC hygiene status affects disease susceptibility. A dirty character in a crowded city is a disease vector.

enemy_behavior_system.philosophy: Everyone operates on schedules and does things that make sense for their archetype and profession. Enemies are not just combat encounters.

bandit_behavior.motivations: Economic desperation, political displacement, organized crime operation
bandit_behavior.territory: Bandits hold territory. Eliminating one group creates a vacancy that may be filled.
bandit_behavior.intelligence: Bandits have information networks. They know the road and what travels it.

enemy_archetypes.behavior: Opportunistic, risk-averse, will flee if outmatched
enemy_archetypes.schedule: Lurks near roads, markets, isolated travelers. Rests in hidden camps.
Patrols territory, collects 'tolls', rests in fortified camp

enemy_archetypes.behavior: Paranoid, skilled (former soldier), dangerous when cornered
enemy_archetypes.schedule: Avoiding settlements, moving frequently, stealing to survive

enemy_archetypes.behavior: Disciplined within unit, follows rules of engagement (usually)
enemy_archetypes.schedule: Patrol routes, garrison duty, campaign operations

enemy_archetypes.behavior: Predictable once understood. Seasonal patterns. Does not hate you personally.
enemy_archetypes.schedule: Dawn and dusk activity peaks. Territory defined. Seasonal migration.

enemy_behavior_system.finite_enemy_population: Enemies are drawn from the world's finite population pool. Killing bandits removes them permanently. New bandits come from the population pool — displaced people, desperate people, organized criminal recruitment.

time_system.time_passes_realtime: The world clock runs whether the player is active or resting.
time_system.npc_schedules_run: All NPC schedules execute in real time.
time_system.faction_events_run: Political and economic events occur on their own timelines.
time_system.player_can_sleep: Time-skipping through sleep is normal. World continues.
time_system.offline_question: Whether world simulation runs while the player is completely offline is a design decision to be made later.

legacy_system.permadeath_philosophy: You are not special. You died. The world adjusted.
what_persists_after_death: Property owned — shop, farm, home — remains in world, may be inherited, sold, or claimed
Reputation — NPCs remember the character if they knew them. 'Old Aldric's forge, still running.'
Faction relationships — organizations remember. A mercenary company may mourn a fallen member or hold a grudge against their killer.
Economic changes — a successful merchant character left economic ripples. A failed lord left a power vacuum.
Named legacy — in rare cases (significant achievement), the character may be referenced in NPC dialogue, oral tradition, or documents
legacy_system.world_reshaping: The next playthrough inherits a world slightly reshaped by the previous character's absence. Small ripples, not dramatic rewrites.
